risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "A list was produced by the Nottingham Clinical Trials Unit to allow simple (unrestricted) randomization (Schulz & Grimes, 2002). Once potential participants had completed the screening questions... If participants consented, they were randomized by the portal designers at ANU." Comment: The study describes a formal randomization process using a pre-produced list from a Clinical Trials Unit for simple unrestricted randomization, implemented through the trial portal. This provides sufficient detail to indicate a proper random allocation sequence was used.

Quote: "The MoodGYM developers were commissioned by the study investigators to construct a research portal that could allocate login identities, screen, take consent, randomize and direct participants to the appropriate arm of the study." and "If participants consented, they were randomized by the portal designers at ANU. In this way the randomization status of participants was concealed from their employers and from the research team until the study was completed." Comment: This is an explicit description of a central/automated web-based allocation process, with allocation performed by the research portal and concealed from the employers and research team, so the person enrolling participants could not foresee assignments before enrolment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The study design aimed to be double-blind. Participants were told that they were participating in a trial of ‘online self-help’, comparing two approaches... However, the telephone interviewers who recorded service use measures for economic analysis were not blind to the randomization status of the participants." Comment: Blinding was attempted for participants, but the interventions were materially different (interactive MoodGYM modules versus informational websites), so personnel delivering weekly telephone contact were at least partly unblinded, as explicitly stated. The study’s main outcomes were subjective and self-reported (e.g. "subjective, work-related performance" on the WSAS, PHQ-9, CORE10, GAD, self-assessed absence from work), which could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of allocation. Therefore there is positive evidence of incomplete blinding with outcomes susceptible to performance bias.

Quote: "The study design aimed to be double-blind. Participants were told that they were participating in a trial of ‘online self-help’, comparing two approaches, and researcher bias was avoided completely because most data for the outcome measures were collected online. However, the telephone interviewers who recorded service use measures for economic analysis were not blind to the randomization status of the participants." Comment: Outcome assessors were mainly the participants themselves via online self-report (WSAS primary outcome; PHQ-9, CORE10, GAD, EQ-5D, self-assessed absence from work). The report states participants were blinded to which approach was the intervention by being told only that two online self-help approaches were being compared, so self-reported subjective outcomes were assessed under participant blinding. Although telephone interviewers collecting service-use/economic outcomes were unblinded, those outcomes were not the most vulnerable subjective efficacy outcomes; the main subjective outcomes were self-reported online under the study's double-blind design.

Quote: "Completion was 359/637 (56%) at 6 weeks and 231/637 (36%) at 12 weeks." and "At 6 weeks the proportion completing follow-up was higher for the control arm but this difference was not statistically significant." and Table 3: "WSAS ... 6 weeks 188 [control] ... 171 [MoodGYM]; 12 weeks 129 [control] ... 102 [MoodGYM]." Comment: The analysed outcomes had very high post-randomization loss: for the primary WSAS outcome, 278/637 (44%) were missing at 6 weeks and 406/637 (64%) at 12 weeks, well above the ~30% threshold. Attrition was also somewhat higher in MoodGYM than control (6 weeks: 46.2% vs 41.1%; 12 weeks: 67.9% vs 59.6%), and the paper notes more losses from MoodGYM. Although the authors used mixed-effects models with maximum likelihood and explored variables associated with missingness, they also state "No imputation was conducted on outcome scores," and the reported attrition on the outcome remains substantial enough to plausibly bias results.
